text: BURTONWOOD, a chapelry in the p. of Warrington, Lancashire, 31 m. SW of Newton-in-Makerfield, near the Liverpool and Manchester railway and the Sankey canal. Area 4,250 acres. Pop. in 1841, 836.
BURTRASK, a town of Sweden, in the prov. and 50 m. NNE of Umea, and 22 m. NW of Lofanger.

BURU, one of the Molucca islands, in the Ceram group. It is about 75 m. in length, and 35 m. in breadth; and is said to be exceedingly fertile, producing large quantities of rice and sago, and many kinds of beautiful cabinet-wood. The pop., which has been estimated at 60,000, chiefly consists of Moors and Alforas. On the NE coast, in S lat. 3° 24', E long. 12° 4', is a spacious harbour, and a Dutch fort.
BURUDDA. See BARODA.
BURUHAN, a town on the coast of the island of Samar, in the Philippine group, in N lat. 11° 40'.
BURUJERD, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Irak, 40 m. from Hissar. Pop. 12,000.
BURUJON, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 12 m. WNW of Toledo, near the N bank of the Tagus.
BURUNJUK, a district of Khiva. lying on the S coast of the Mertovi-Kultuk, or Dead bay of the Caspian, in the Usturt.
BURUT, a town of Afghanistan, in the district of Shoruwuk, 125 m. SSW of Kandahar.

BURWA, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, district and about 39 m. NE of Kuttack, in BUR the vicinity of which is the ferry of the Kursora, and a fine Mogul bridge across the Narussia. BURWA, Burwah, or locally Hazary Baugh, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, district and 87 m. WSW of Ramgur, on the l. bank of the Soauk river. It is the head-quarters of the district Sepoy corps.
BURWALLA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, district of Arratum, 50 m. WSW of Cambay, on the l. bank of the Outouly.

BURWANI, a town of Hindostan, in the district of the same name, prov. of Kandeish, 30 m. SW of Maundu. It is surrounded by a double wall and ditch, and contains a gurry and a palace 6 stories in height, but both much dilapidated. The district running along the S bank of the Nerbudda is about 65 m. in length, and 45 m. in breadth, intersected by the Satpura range of mountains.
BURWARDSLEY, a chapelry in Cheshire, 43 m. SSW of Tarporley. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 458.
BURWARTON, a parish in Salop, 10 m. NE of Ludlow. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 151.
BURWASH, a parish in Sussex, 6 m. SE by S of Wadhurst. Area 6,840 acres. Pop. 2,093.
BURWELL, a parish in Lincolnshire, 6 m. SSE of Louth. Area 2,190 acres. Pop. 174.—Also two united parishes and a village in Cambridgeshire, 4 m. NW by W of Newmarket. Area 6,505 acres.
BURWELL BAY, a village in Isle of Wight co., Virginia, 69 m. SE of Richmond.

BURWELL LAKE, a lake in the township of Bosanquet, in the W district of Canada West, 3 m. from Lake Huron. It is about 4 m. in length, by 2 m. in breadth; and is bordered by a considerable extent of marsh land.
BURWHA, a town of Bornu, on the W shore of Lake Tchad, in N lat. 13° 51'.

BURY, a parish and parliamentary burgh in the co.-palatine of Lancaster; 194 m. NNW of London; 8 m. NNW of Manchester; and 44 m. SSE of Lancaster; on the E bank of the Irwell; comprising the townships of Coupe-Lench Newhall, Elton, Henheads, Musbury, Walmersley, and the chapelries of Heap, Tottington Higher and Tottington Lower. Area of p. 22,600 acres. Pop. in 1801, 24,482; in 1831, 47,829; in 1841, 62,125. Pop. of the township in 1801, 7,072; in 1831, 15,086; in 1841, 20,710. Assessed property of township in 1815, £16,546; in 1842-3, £62,178. The ancient staple manufacture of woollen is still carried on here; but the great manufacture is that of cotton. In 1838 there were 17 woollen mills here, with 888 hands; and 114 cotton mills, in which 13,652 hands were engaged. The large calico-printing and other establishments, formerly belonging to Sir R. Peel, extend a considerable way along the banks of the Irwell; there are also large bleaching-grounds in the neighbourhood.—The general appearance of this town is rapidly improving by the widening, paving, and lighting of its streets, and the removal of many old dilapidated buildings. It possesses a public subscription library, a news-room, a botanical institution, a mechanics' library, a medical library, and a savings' bank. The canal from this town to Manchester conduces very materially to its trading prosperity; and the East Lancashire line connects it with Manchester and Liverpool on the one hand, and with Accrington, Burnley, Blackburn, and Preston, on the other. Some very important improvements in different arts have been made here. In 1738 John Kay, a native of B., but at the time residing at Colchester, invented a new mode of throwing the shuttle, by means of the picking-peg; and in 1760 his son, Robert, invented the drop-box, by which the weaver can, at pleasure, use any one of three shuttles. The invention of setting cards by machinery also belongs to this place. This town now returns one member to parliament. The number of electors in 1847 was 903. The government of B. is vested in three constables, chosen annually at 3,516. a court-leet held under the Earl of Derby. B. is a here. place of considerable antiquity, although its present importance is of modern origin. In the Magna Britannia, published in 1738, it is described as "a little market-town." By some it is supposed to have been a Roman station.

BURY, a parish in Huntingdonshire, 1 m. S of Ramsey. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 359.—Also a p. in Sussex, 4 m. N of Arundel. Area 3,340 acres. Pop. 611.—Also a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 9 m. SW of Ath. Pop. 679.

BURY-ST.-EDMUNDS, a parliamentary borough and market-town, in the co. of Suffolk, situated on the W side of the Larke, in the midst of a richly cultivated tract of country, 31 m. NW of Ipswich, and 743 m. NE of London. Area of p. 3,040 acres. Pop. in 1801, 7,655; in 1831, 11,436; in 1841, 12,538. The town is well-built, and contains some handsome public edifices. The most remarkable buildings are the shire-hall, on the site of the ancient church of St. Margaret, the guild-hall, wool-hall, the market-cross, and theatre. The church of St. Mary is a fine Gothic structure of the 15th cent., with an exquisitely carved roof. The belfry of St. James is one of the noblest specimens of Norman art in the kingdom. The only manufacture carried on in B. is one of light woollen stuffs, lately established in the workhouse. The chief business is the wool-trade. The town is connected with the Eastern Union railway by a line to Ipswich, 26 m. in length, which was opened in December 1846. The Larke becomes navigable to Lynn for barges about 1 m. from the town. This burgh sent members to parliament in the 30th Edward I., but not afterwards until the 18th of James I., since which time it has always returned two representatives. The number of voters registered in 1837 was 665; in 1847, 763.—B. was a place of considerable importance previous to the introduction of Christianity into Britain, and is thought to have been the Villa Faustina of the Romans. In Saxon times it was named Beodric's worthe, or 'the dwelling of Beodric,' whose possession it was at the time of the heptarchy, and by whom it was bequeathed to Edmund, who succeeded his uncle Offa, as king of the East Angles, and being murdered by the Danes, was canonized as a martyr. It was from this St. Edmund, or rather from a monastery founded here in honour of the royal martyr, that the town derived its present name. This town shares with Runymede the honour of producing Magna charta, a meeting of the barons having been convened here to deliberate on the charter of Henry I. During the reign of the Plantagenets, several parliaments were held here. The celebrated John Lydgate, translator of Boccaccio's Latin poem, 'De casibus Virorum et Fæminarum Illustrium,' was born here. It was also the native place of the able but cruel Bishop Gardner; and of jak and SW of Semendria. Humphry Repton, the celebrated landscape gardener.
BURYAN (St.), a parish in Cornwall, 5 m. SW of Penzance. Area 6,970 acres. Pop. 1,911.
BURYTHORPE, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. S of New Molton. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 226.

BURZEN, a river of Transylvania, an affluent of the Aluta, into which it falls, after a course of 35 m., 1,360. at Brenndorf. It gives name to the valley through which it flows, which is called the Burzenland.
BURZENIN, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Kalitsch, in the obwod and 12 m. SSE of Sieradz, on the I. bank of the Warta. Pop. 500. BUS

BURZET, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, arrond. of Largentière.-The cant., comprising 4 com., had a pop. of 5,878 in 1831. -The com., 12 m. N of Largentière, has a pop. of There are silk and woollen manufactories
BUSACH, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, on the l. bank of the Glener, 2 m. S of Vrin, and 26 m. SW of Chur.
BUSACHI, a prefetture and town in the island of Sardinia, 22 m. NE of Oristano. Pop. of t. 1,887.

BUSACO, a convent in Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, on a ridge 22 m. N of Coimbra, called the Sierra de Busaco, extending N from the river Mondego. The monks are of the Carmelite order, and their system is to a great extent modelled on that of La Trappe. The prospect from the highest point of the convent is one of the most extensive in Portugal; but that which gives the place a title to notice is the memorable action fought here on 27th September, 1810, between Massena and Wellington. The superior force of the French had obliged the British general to retire from Almeida, and to contemplate a farther retreat upon Lisbon, availing himself, however, of occasional opportunities of resisting in favourable positions. Of this description was the Sierra de Busaco; and two desperate attacks here made by the French on the retreating forces,—the one on the right, the other on the left of the highest ground,-proved totally unsuccessful. The right, consisting chiefly of British troops, allowed the enemy to come within a short distance, after which they pushed forward, and drove them from the ground. On the left the Portuguese bore a large share in the honour of the repulse. The loss on the British and Portuguese side, in killed and wounded, was nearly 1,200 men; that of the enemy is supposed to have been three times as many; but the latter being still greatly superior in numbers detached a force to march round the N extremity of the mountain, in the hope of cutting off the allied troops from Coimbra, whereupon Wellington retreated first on that city, and afterwards to the lines of Torres Vedras.
BUSACQUINO, a town and district of Sicily, in the district and 8 m. SW of Corleone. Pop. 8,000.
BUSAIDA, a village on the coast of Tripoli, in N lat. 31°, E long. 17° 40′.
BUSBACH, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, in the circle and 6 m. E of Aix-la-Chapelle. Pop. 1,000. Lead and calamine are mined in the vicinity.
BUSBY (GREAT and LITTLE), two townships in the p. of Stokesley, in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 23 m. S of Stokesley. Area 2,090 acres. Pop. of Great B., 114; of Little B., 34.
BUSCA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 12 m. NW of Cuneo, on the river Maira, at an alt. of 1,374 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 8,500.
BUSCEMI, a town of Sicily, in the district of Noto, on the slope of Monte Laura. Pop. 600.
BUSCHALOWATZ, a town of Servia, in the san-
BUSCOT, a parish in Berks, 4 m. NW by W of Great Farringdon, on the Thames. Area 2,910 acres. Pop. 405.
BUSEA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, district and 55 m. W of Sirhind.
BUSECK, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the circle and 4 m. E of Giessen, on the Wieseck. Pop.
BUSEMPRA. See BOOSEMPRA.
BUSENWORT, a village of Denmark, in the duchy of Holstein, near the coast, 8 m. N of the entrance of the Elbe.
BUSEO, a district and village of Wallachia, on a river of the same name, an affluent of the Sereth. ! Pop. of district 4,500.
BUSH, a small river of co. Antrim. It rises on the S side of Slievenory, and has a WNW course of about 25 m. to the sea at Port Ballentrae.
BUSHBURY, a parish in Staffordshire, 3 m. N by E of Wolverhampton. Area 7,610 acres. Pop. 1,509.
BUSH CREEK, a town in Washington co., in the state of Arkansas, U. S. Pop. 298.—Also a town in Sciota eo., in Ohio. Pop. 401.
BUSH ISLAND, a low sandy island, being a reclaimed sand-bank, in the mouth of the Yang-tse- Kiang river, on the coast of China, opposite the junction of the Whangpu.
BUSHEAB, an island in the Persian gulf, in N lat. 26° 50'. It is about 12 m. in length, and from 4 to 5 m. broad, and runs parallel to the coast at a distance of from 3 to 4 leagues.

BUSHEHR, or BUSHIRE, a town and well-frequented harbour of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, on the Persian gulf, in N lat. 29°, W long. 50° 51', 120 m. WSW of Shiraz. It occupies the N extremity of a sandy peninsula, 11 m. long, and 4 m. broad; and is encircled on all sides but the S by water. In high tides and storms it has sometimes been completely insulated. Vessels drawing above 18 ft. cannot enter the harbour, but remain in the roads, where the anchorage, protected from the N by the island of Karak and the coast, is good. The town is of a triangular form, and is fortified on the land side by a mud wall, mounting a few pieces of cannon. The streets are very narrow; and, although presenting a handsome appearance from a distance, B. is but a mean place in reality. The principal dwellings are all flat-roofed and terraced, and there are no domes or minarets seen among them. The town stands on a very slight eminence, shelving gently down to the beach on either hand. One-third of the houses are mere unroofed reed enclosures. Provisions and fruits are cheap and excellent; but the water must be brought from a distance of 2 m.; nor is it to be obtained of a good quality nearer than 16 m. The heat also is intense; and the climate, especially in the fall of the year, very unhealthy. B. is the principal entrepot for the supply of Persia with Indian goods; and likewise receives all goods coming from Europe and destined for the S parts of the empire. The exports are Persian commodities, such as carpets, silks, horses, wine of Shiraz, rose-water, drugs; and the imports are Indian goods of different kinds, sugar, steel, tobacco, and English broad cloth. The English East India company have a factory at this place, and the resident possesses considerable influence in the town. The resident merchants are chiefly Persians and Armenians. The pop.—"a disagreeable mixture of the Arab and the Persian" [Buckingham]—is variously estimated at from 8,000 to 15,000. The common language is a bad dialect of Persian. A plain or belt of land about 40 m. in width occupies the space between B. and the chain of mountains which intervenes between it and Shiraz. The whole of this plain has the climate and vegetation of Arabia, the only objects to be seen upon it being palm-trees; but in the gardens about 3 m. from B. the soil begins to be arable, and aloes, pomegranates, and orange-trees are met with. - Fraser. - Kinneir.—Buckingham.—Fontanier.
BUSHEY, a parish in Hertfordshire, 2 m. SE by E of Watford. Area 3,130 acres. Pop. 2,675.

BUSHI, a district on the r. bank of the Niger, extending between Bachinku and Egga. It comprises about 40 towns or villages; and the pop., said to amount to 30,000, are represented as an industrious people, who weave excellent cotton cloth.
BUSHKILL, a township in Northampton co., in BUS the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., on a creek of the same name flowing into the Delaware. Pop. 1,716.
BUSHKURD. See Beluchistan, p. 670.

BUSHLEY, a parish in Worcestershire, 2 m. NNW of Tewkesbury. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. 334. BUSHMEN, Boschmen, or Bosjesmens, a wild race of people in the W part of Southern Africa, who roam over the immense plains to the N of Cape colony. They appear to be a branch very anciently separated from the Hottentots. Hunger and cold, and every species of privation and distress, have cramped their growth, and dwindled them down to a stature the most diminutive; the middle size of the men being about 4 ft. 6 inches; and of the other sex 4 ft.; many are several inches below this standard. They are hideously ugly in shape and feature. The outline of the face is triangular and concave; the cheek-bones are high; the chin and forehead sharp and prominent; the nose is flat,—the lips are thick and projecting,—the eyes small, obliquely placed in the head, narrow, sunk, keen, and always in motion,— their colour is a dirty yellow, or that of a withered tobacco leaf, and is usually concealed by a coating of dirt and grease,—their legs, thighs, and arms, are lean, withered, and divested of all appearance of muscle,-while the joints are large, and the belly is protuberant! The clothing of the men by day and night consists of a raw skin of a sheep, goat, or antelope, to which the women add a belt of the same material; to this is appended in front another piece of skin cut into narrow thongs. They sometimes wear round their ancies twisted thongs of skins, and have bits of copper, or shells, or glass beads round their neck, or dangling from the curling tufts of their greasy hair,—hair, unlike that of any other human beings, growing in little detached pellets on the scalp. Every B. carries a small bow of about 2 ft. 6 inches in length, with a quiver on his back filled with barbed arrows about 18 in. in length, and poisoned by the juice of the Illiteris bulb; these, when he sallies forth to fight or plunder, are stuck in a fillet of skin round the head; and he has generally thrust through the cartilage of his nose a piece of wood or a porcupine-quill. The hut of a B. is nothing more than a mat of rushes or tong-grass, bent between two sticks into a semicircular shape over a hollow in the ground, scooped out like the nest of the ostrich, in which he coils himself round when he lies down to sleep; frequently his only abode is the shelter afforded by the rocks or caverns of the mountains. Their language consists of snapping, hissing, grunting nasal sounds. Yet they are said to be a keen-witted race, and to exhibit an extraordinary talent for mimicry. Communities or families, of a character similar to what we understand by the term Bushmen, inhabit all the barren wastes of Great Namaqualand. To the north of these miserable people, several missionary stations have been occupied with considerable success, and by means of these missions we have been made acquainted with a considerable tract of country formerly unknown
BUSHMILLS, a small market-town, in the parishes of Dunluce and Billy, in co. Antrim, on the Bush river, 61 m. NE of Coleraine. Pop. 788.
BUSHWANAS. See BECHUANAS.
BUSHWICK, a township in King's co., in the state of New York, U. S., 3 m. E of Brooklyn, on Newtown creek. Pop. 1,295.

BUSHY ISLAND, an island in Clarence strait, on the NW coast of America, in N lat. 56° 17', W long. 132° 42'.—Also an island on the W coast of Van Diemen's Land, \(\frac{1}{3}\) m. N of Mount Wellington, at the entrance of Macquarie harbour.
BUSI, an island of the Adriatic, off the coast of Dalmatia, 8 m. SW of Lissa, in N lat. 42° 57', E long. 16° 1′. Pop. 100.
BUSI, or Borst, one of the Bissagos islands on the W coast of Africa, adjacent to Bisao, on the W side. It is thickly wooded, and inhabited by a very fierce tribe of Papels.
BUSIGNY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Clari. Pop. 2,275.
BUSIR. See ABUSIR.
BUSIR-BENA, a village in the Delta of Egypt, near Sebennytus, in the neighbourhood of which are some traces of the ancient Busiris.
BUSK, a village of Austria, in Galicia, 18 m. NE of Lemberg, on the Bug. Pop. 2,981, of whom about 500 are Jews. There are iron-furnaces in the vicinity.

BUSKERUD, or BUSKEREDS, a bailiwick or administrative division of Norway, in the dio. of Aggerhuus; bounded on the N by the amts or bailiwicks of Nordre Bergenhuus and Christiana; on the E by Aggerhuus; on the S by Jarlsberg and Bradsberg; and on the W by Sondre Bergenhuus. It has an area of 109 Swedish or 4,790 English sq. m.; with a | to different parts of the country. A mine of argenpop. in 1833, of 76,669; in 1845, of 74,083. Its N portion comprises Hallingdalen, or the vale of the Hallingdals river. On its S skirts rise the Hallings- Karven, alt. 6,017 ft.; the Tessung-field, 5,000 ft.; and the Blee-field. The Eggedals-fillen mountains divide the basin of the Hallingdals on the N, from that of the Lauven on the S. The principal towns are Drammen and Kongsberg.
BUSKIRK'S BRIDGE, a village in Cambridge township, Washington co., New York, U. S., 29 m. N by E of Albany, on the Hoosick river. Pop. 200.
BUSKO, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Kielce, obwod of Stopnica, 45 m. NE of Cracow. Pop. 800. There are mineral springs and baths here.
BUSLINGTHORPE, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. SW by W of Market-Raisin. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. 50.
BUSLYAN, a village of Turkey in Asia, in the prov. of Karamania, in the sanjak and 35 m. NNE of Kasaria, on a tributary of the Kisil-Irmak. It is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Sacana.
BUSNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Lilliers. Pop. 1,488.
BUSO (CAPE), an elevated and bluff point, forming the NW extremity of the island of Candia, in N Iat. 35° 36′ 30″, E long. 23° 35′.

BUSPA, a river of India, in Kunawur, flowing through "the most romantic of the Himalayan valleys," in a WNW direction to the Upper Sutledge. It rises in snowy mountains of immense height, through which lies the Chungsakhago pass, in N lat. 32° 14'. At Chetkul, the highest v. in the valley, 25 m. below its source, its bed has an elevation of 11,400 ft. above sea-level. At Rakcham, 6 m. below Chetkul, its elevation is 10,456 ft. Here the breadth of the dell is from \( \frac{1}{4} \) to \( \frac{1}{6} \) m., and the level space is often thickly clad with wood, chiefly pines, willows, hazel, and sweetbriar. Below this, at Sungla, its elevation is 8,568 ft.; and at Sango, near its junction with the Sutledge, it is 5,945 ft. The lower parts of the valley are neatly laid out in fields of pease, beans, and turnips; and the B. glides along an expanded bed of sand and pebbles, in which it forms numerous islets, overgrown with barberries and willows. As it approaches the Sutledge, the B. becomes hemmed in between bold mural cliffs, shaded by pines of luxuriant growth. The entire length of the valley of the B. is about 40 m.— Gerard.

BUSSAHER, a principality of Northern Hindostan; bounded on the NW by the Sutledge river; on the N and NE by China; on the SE and S by the Jumna river; and on the SW by Sirmor. It lies between the parallels of 32° and 36° N; and between 77° and 79° E long.; and is divided into the districts of Kunawur, Rampore, Paber, and Dasau. The cap., Rampore, is situated in N lat. 31° 27', E long. 77° 38', on the l. bank of the Sutledge. A great part of the country is so greatly elevated as to be covered with snow and wholly incapable of culture. In the lower districts wheat and barley are raised; but the chief employment of the pop. is breeding and rearing sheep, goats, and mules; and woollen cloths, coarse blanketings, and shawls are manufactured. The majority of the inhabitants are Hindus. -The Gorkhas wrested this territory from its rajah or superior feudal chief, in 1810. In 1814, he was reinstated in his authority by a British force.

BUSSANG, or BILTZENBACH, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Ramonchamp, near the source of the Moselle, and 20 m. SE of Remiremont. Pop. 2,349. It possesses some cotton manufactories, and in the vicinity are mineral waters, 20,000 bottles of which are annually exported BUS tiferous copper exists also in the locality.
BUSSE. See BISATO.
BUSSEAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar and zemendary of Chuta Nagpoor, on the l. bank of the Coyle, 52 m. SE of Burwa.
BUSSEE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, dist. and 3 m. N of Sirhind.
BUSSEAU (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Coulonges. Pop. 1,095.

BUSSERACH, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 20 m. N of Solothurn. Pop. (Catholic) 601. It contains the ruins of the ancient fortress of Thierstein, belonging to the family of that name.
BUSSEROLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Bussiere-Badil, near the Trieux. Pop. 2,187. It has some iron-works.
BUSSET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Cusset. Pop. 1,689.
BUSSETO, or Bussetto, a town of Parma, cap. of the cant. of the same name, in the dist. and 7 m. N of Borgo-San-Donino, near the Ongino. Pop. 2,000. In the vicinity is an ancient abbey.
BUSSI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra, dist. and 26 m. ESE of Aquila. Pop. 1,300. It contains a church, a royal abbey, and manufactories of fine china and cloth.
BUSSIERE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Nerode. Pop. 1,624.
BUSSIERE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Briare. Pop. 610.-Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of St. Savin. Pop. 1,096.

BUSSIERE-BADIL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, arrond. of Nontron. The cant. comprises 8 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 8,199. The town is 10 m. N of Nontron. Pop. 1,191. It possesses extensive brick and tile-works.
BUSSIERE-BOFFY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Mezieres. Pop. 1,139.
BUSSIERE-DUNOISE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, cant. of Saint-Vaury. Pop. 2,811.
BUSSIERE-GALANT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Chalus. Pop. 1,580.

BUSSIERE-LA-GRUE, a village of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. and 11 m. SSW of Bourbonl'Archambault, on the Morgan. Pop. 1,560. It has manufactories of potato-starch, and in the vicinity is a mine of iron.
BUSSIERE-POITEVINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Mezieres, 12 m. NNW of Mezieres. Pop. 2,045. Fairs are held monthly.

BUSSIERES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saône-et-Loire, cant. of Macon. Pop. 422. The locality affords good wine.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of La Ferté-sous-Jonarre. Pop. 407.
BUSSIERES-LES-BELMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. of Fays-Billot. Pop. 1,817. Baskets form the chief article of local manufacture.
BUSSNANG, or Busslingen, a parish and village of Switzerland, cap. of a circle in the cant. of Thurgau, bail. and 2 m. SE of Weinfelden, on the l. bank of the Thur. Pop. of parish 2,012.

BUSSOLENGO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 8 m. WNW of Verona, on the r. bank of the Adige. Pop. 2,331. It is well-built, and possesses some linen manufactories, and an active transit trade. Fairs are held twice a-year.

BUSSOLIN, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento, in the prov. and 8 m. E of Susa, on the r. bank of the Doire, which is here crossed by the road to Turin. Pop. 1,000. In the adjacent mountain of Faussemagne are the quarries of green marble known as the Susa marble.
BUSSORA. See BASSORA.
BUSSORAH, or Bussra. See Bassora.
BUSSUNDAR, or Dooloo, (DALU BASSANDRA), a village of Hindostan, in Nepaul, on a tributary of the Godra, 36 m. SW of Jemla. It is a celebrated place of Hindu pilgrimage.
BUSSY, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 9 m. W of Lausanne, on the Geneva road.
BUSSUTH. See Bossuth.
BUSSY-LA-COTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, cant. of Revigny. Pop. 195. The environs afford good wine.
BUSSY-LE-GRAND, a commune of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, cant. of Flavigny, 12 m. NE of Semur. Pop. 850. It contains a castle, ironworks, and a fuller's mill.

BUSSY-EN-OTHE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Brienon, 6 m. NE of Joigny, and 17 m. N of Auxerre, near the forest of Othe. The environs afford wine, varieties of fruit and rosin, and contain numerous lime-kilns and tile and brick-works.
BUSSY-LA-PESLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, cant. of Brinon-les-Allemands, on a stream of the same name, which flows into the Loze. Pop. 227.

BUSSY - SAINT - GEORGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Lagny, 25 m. from Meaux. Pop. 538. It contains an agricultural establishment in which flocks of the merino species are extensively reared.
BUSSY-SAINT-MARTIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Lagny, 15 m. from Lagny. Pop. 294. It contains a fine agricultural establishment.
BUSTABECK, a township in the p. of Castle- Sowerby, in Cumberland, 4 m. NE of Hesketh-Newmarket. Pop. 249.

BUSTAR, or Bastar, a district and town of India, in Gundwana. The town is in N lat. 19° 31', 170 m. from Gonjam. The district is a sterile tract covered with jungly hills and pestilential morasses; and inhabited by a fierce tribe of Gonds.
BUSTARD. See OUTARDE.
BUSTARD BAY, a bay on the E coast of New Holland, in S lat. 24° 5′, E long. 151° 42′.
BUSTI, a township in Chatanque co., in the state of New York, U. S., 323 m. W by S of Albany. Pop. 1,894.
BUSTO-ARSEZIO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. and 24 m. NW of Milan. Pop. 7,000. It has some cotton manufactories.
BUSTON (High and Low), two townships in the p. of Warkworth, Northumberland, 4 m. SE of Alnwick. Pop. of High B. 100; of Low B. 115.
BUSUM, a village of Denmark, in Holstein, near the coast, 12 m. SW of Heyde.
BUSWAGON, one of the Philippine islands in the Calamaine group, in about 12° N lat., E long. 120° 20'. It is about 50 m. in length, and 13 m. in breadth.

BUTAN, or BHUTAN, an extensive region of N Hindostan, bounded on the N and E by Tibet, from which it is separated by a branch of the Himalayas, and on the S by Assam and Bengal. It lies chiefly between the 26th and 28th parallels of N lat., and measures about 250 m. from E to W, with an average | the Rhine, circle and NE of Dusseldorf. BUT breadth from N to S of 90 m. Forming the S declivity of the great table-land of Tibet, it is altogether a mountainous region; and though situated within the tropics, from its great elevation, in many districts experiences the rigour of an arctic climate; but the lower districts are productive and highly cultivated, and the fruits both of Europe and Asia are produced within the space of a few miles. The ruler of this territory, or deb raja, as he is called, is tributary to the grand lama of Tibet. The low country is governed by hereditary chiefs who pay a fixed contribution to the deb raja. The Bhotias are a tall, athletic race, with dark complexion, high cheekbones, black hair, and narrow pointed eyes, -a mixture apparently of Chinese and Tartars. They have little intercourse with their southern neighbours; but the deb raja sends an annual caravan to Rangpur, with coarse woollen cloths, wax, ivory, gold dust, tea, paper, and horses, for which returns are made chiefly in fine English woollens, cottons, indigo, frankincense, nutmegs, and gunpowder. The amount of this traffic does not exceed £4,000 per annum. The cap. of B. is Tasisudon, in N lat. 27° 5', E long. 89° 40'. Dalimcoth or Dellamcotta, is the key of the defile by which B. can be entered from Bengal.
BUTANG, or Bouton, an island in the strait of Malacca, 35 m. from the Malay coast, in N lat. 6° 33', and E long. 99° 17'.
BUTCOMBE, a parish in Somersetshire, 8 m. NE of Axbridge. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 256.

BUTE, an island in the frith of Clyde, separated from the district of Cowal, in Argyllshire, by a very narrow channel called the Kyles of B. It is in length about 16 m.; and from 3 to 5 m. in breadth. Its general direction is from SE to NW. The N parts of the island are rocky and barren; but the S extremity is fertile, well-cultivated, and enclosed. The bays of Rothesay, Kames, and Kilchattan, indent its E shore; those of Stravannan, Scalpsie, Ettrick, and Kilmichael, its W. Stravannan bay, and that of Kilchattan, run so far inland as to make the S end of B. an oval peninsula. Near the middle of the island are several small lochs. Port Bannatyne, on the bay of Kames, 2 m. NE of Rothesay, is a pleasant hamlet. The climate, though damp, is mild and temperate, and a considerable portion of the soil is arable. Freestone of a reddish colour abounds in the island, and limestone is met with in every part of it. Its only town is Rothesay, which is also the chief town of the shire of Bute. B. gives the title of Marquess to a branch of the family of Stuart, who is proprietor of the greater part of the island. Pop. in 1791, 6,470; in 1801, 6,106; in 1831, 6,830; in 1841, 9,499.
BUTERA, a town of Sicily, in the district and 12 m. NNW of Terranova. Pop. 4,081.

BUTESHIRE, a county of Scotland, composed of the islands of Arran, Bute, the Cumbraes, and INCHMARNOCK. See these articles separately described. There are five parishes and one royal burgh within this shire. Pop. in 1801, 11,791; in 1831, 14,200; in 1841, 15,740. Assessed property in 1815, £22,541; in 1842-3, £31,161. The number of parochial schools in 1834 was 10; of schools not parochial, 30; total number of scholars, 2,354. The county returns one member to parliament. Parliamentary constituency in 1842-3, 421. The sheriff and small-debt courts are held at Rothesay.
BUTGENBACH, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle of Malmedy. Pop. 395.
BUTI, a parish and town of Tuscany, in the district and 8 m. E of Pisa. Pop. 3,498. The oil grown in the environs of this place is reputed to be the best in Tuscany.
BUTINGEN, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of
BUTLEIGH, a parish in Somersetshire, 4 m. SSE of Glastonbury. Area 4,670 acres. Pop. 872, of whom 212 were in the hamlet of Butleigh-Wooton.

BUTLER, a county in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., skirting in the NE and SE on the Alleghany river. Area 800 sq. m. Pop. 22,378. Cap., Butler, 203 m. NNW of Harrisburg. Pop. 861.—Also a county in the S part of Alabama, watered by branches of the Conecuh. Area 1,000 sq. m. Pop. 8,685. Cap., Greenville.—Also a county in the W part of Kentucky, on the Green river. Area 570 sq. m. Pop. 3,898. Cap., Morgantown. - Also a county in the SW of Ohio, watered by the Miami. Area 480 sq. m. Pop. 28,173. Cap., Hamilton.—Also a township in Wayne co., in New York, 178 m. WNW of Albany. Pop. 2,271.—Also a township in Butler co., Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,384.—Also a township in Luzerne co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 514.—Also a township in Branch co., in Michigan. Pop. 234.— Also a township in Columbiana co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,711.—Also a township in Dark co., Ohio. Pop. 1,116.—Also a township in Montgomery co., Ohio. Pop. 1,896.
BUTLERS-MARSTON, a parish in Warwickshire, 11 m. SW of Kington. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 313.

BUTLEY, a township in the p. of Prestbury, in Cheshire, 3 m. N by W of Macclesfield. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 602.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 6 m. E by N of Woodbridge, on the W side of the estuary of the Butley. Area 1,690 acres. Pop. 364.
BUTON. See BOUTON.
BUTOT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Pavilly, 14 m. N of Rouen. Pop. 289. Important cattle-fairs are held here.
BUTOUL, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 64 m. N of Goruckpore, within the Nepalese territory.
BUTOW, or Bytom, a village of Prussia, in Pomerania, in the circle of Lauenburg-Butow, 55 m. E of Koslin. Pop. in 1837, 2,320.
BUTRINTO, a town of Turkey, in Albania, 7 m. NE of Corfu. Pop. 2,000. It occupies the site of the ancient Roman colony of Buthrotum.
BUTSCHOWITZ, a town of Austria, in Moravia, in the circle and 18 m. ESE of Brunn. Pop. 2,473, of whom about 500 were Jews. It has manufactories of woollen cloths, and leather.
BUTSFIELD, a township in the p. of Lanchester, co. and 11 m. NW of Durham, and S of the Stanhope and Tyne railway. Area 1,390 acres. Pop. in 1841, 252.

BUTTELSTADT, a town of Saxe-Weimar, in the circle of Weimar-Jena, 4 m. SW of Buttstadt, and 8 m. N of Weimar, on the Scherkonde. Pop. 832. It contains manufactories of stockings and linen, and several distilleries, and has two annual fairs.
BUTTEN, or Botten, an extensive village of Arabia, in the prov. of Yemen, 40 m. ESE of Chamir, and 60 m. NNW of Sana.
BUTTENHAUSEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, SE of Marbach. Pop. 546.
BUTTENHEIM, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Mayne, 7 m. SSE of Bamburg.
BUTTER-CRAMBE, a township in the p. of | tant) 1,000. Watch-making employs many of the Bossal, N. R. of Yorkshire, 121 m. NE of York, on Area 1,550 acres. Pop. in 1841, the Derwent. 175.
BUTTER-HILL, a mountain of the Fishkill range, in the state of New York, U. S., on the W side of the Hudson, 3 m. below Newburg. Alt. 1,432 ft.
BUTTERLAW, a township in the p. of Newburn, Northumberland, 5 m. WNW of Newcastle-upon- Tyne. Pop. in 1841, 16. BUT
BUTTERLEIGH, a parish of Devon, 2½ m. SE of Tiverton, E of the Exeter river. Area 2,520 acres. Pop. in 1841, 155.

BUTTERLEY, a hamlet in the township of Ripley, p. of Pentrich, co. of Derby, 3 m. S of Alfreston. The iron-works, collieries, and lime-works in this place and the vicinity, employ 1,500 workmen, half of whom are employed in the mines, and the rest in the iron-works and other manufactories. The number of steam-engines used in the works and mines is 26, affording a power equal to that of 700 horses. The Cromford canal affords a medium for the conveyance of goods by water to Cromford, where the High Peak railway commences, extending to Walley-bridge, near Stock-port, where it meets the Stockport junction railway, which connects it with the Manchester and Liverpool line.

BUTTERMERE, a chapelry in the p. of Brigham, Cumberland, 83 m. SW of Keswick, beautifully situated between the lake of the same name and Crummock water. Pop. in 1841, 84. B. lake is a small sheet of water about 11 m. in length, and a 1 m. wide, embosomed amid superb mountain scenery. -Also a p. in Wilts, 5 m. S of Hungerford. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. 130.
BUTTERNUTS, a township in Ocsago co., in the state of New York, U. S., 94 m. W by S of Albany. Pop. 4,057.

BUTTERTON, a chapelry in the p. of Mayfield, Staffordshire, 6 m. E of Lecke, near the Manifold river. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. in 1841, 388.—Also a township in the p. of Trentham, Staffordshire, 2 m. S of Newcastle-under-Lyne, near the Birmingham and Manchester railway, and the Grand Trunk canal.

BUTTERWICK, a township in the p. of Sedgefield, co. and 11 m. SSE of Durham, and S of the Skern. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. in 1841, 51.—Also a parish of Lincolnshire 44 m. E of Boston, on the coast, near Boston Deeps. Area 1,370 acres. Pop. 579.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Foxholes, E. R. of Yorkshire, 10 m. NW of Great Driffield. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 100.—Also a township in the p. of Barton-le-Street, N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NW of New Malton, on the Rye.
BUTTERWICK (East), a township in the p. of Messingham, Lincolnshire, 11 m. W of Glandford- Bridge. Area 680 acres. Pop. 378.
